claims.quote: The mangled forms of millions of martyrs cried to God for vengeance upon that apostate power.
claims.claim_text: Millions of martyrs were killed under the apostate papal power.
claims.extractor_note: The numeric claim ('millions') is embedded in theological framing ('cried to God for vengeance'); the quantitative/historical component is separated out here and flagged for verification.

verification.rationale: The figure of 'millions' of martyrs killed by the papacy reflects 19th-century Protestant martyrology (e.g. John Dowling's History of Romanism, 1847, popularized figures around 'fifty million' killed for heresy) that White's sources carried. Modern historians regard such totals as grossly exaggerated: documented Inquisition executions number in the thousands to low tens of thousands, and even broad tallies of all religious-persecution deaths over the whole medieval-early-modern span fall far short of 'millions' attributable to the papacy specifically. The claim matches period sources but not modern demographic scholarship.
verification.modern_consensus: Executions by the Inquisitions numbered a few thousand to low tens of thousands; 'millions' is considered a large exaggeration.
verification.period_historiography: John Dowling, History of Romanism (1847), and popular Protestant histories cited figures up to ~50 million killed for heresy over the centuries.

sources.title: Were 50 Million People Really Killed in the Inquisition? (National Catholic Register)
sources.note: Position: traces the inflated figure to Dowling (1847) and reports the scholarly estimate of a few thousand executions.

sources.title: Catholic Inquisition Murdered '50-68 Million'? (Patheos / D. Armstrong)
sources.note: Position: argues the multi-million figures are mathematically implausible against medieval European population; cites historians' far lower counts.

claims.quote: Popery had become the world’s despot. Kings and emperors bowed to the decrees of the Roman pontiff. The destinies of men, both for time and for eternity, seemed under his control.
claims.claim_text: By this period, the medieval papacy had become the world's despot, with kings and emperors bowing to the decrees of the Roman pontiff, whose control seemed to extend over the destinies of men both for time and eternity.

verification.rationale: At the height of medieval papal power (notably under Innocent III, r. 1198-1216) the papacy did assert supremacy over secular rulers, humbling kings and emperors (Canossa; John of England's submission; interdicts against kingdoms). The characterization of the papacy dominating temporal and spiritual affairs at its zenith is historically grounded, though 'the world's despot' is rhetorical.

sources.title: Dictatus papae - Wikipedia
sources.note: Papal claims to depose rulers; medieval papal ascendancy over secular power.

claims.quote: For hundreds of years the doctrines of Rome had been extensively and implicitly received, its rites reverently performed, its festivals generally observed. Its clergy were honored and liberally sustained.
claims.claim_text: For hundreds of years, the doctrines of Rome were extensively and implicitly received, its rites reverently performed, its festivals generally observed, and its clergy honored and liberally sustained.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Western Christendom for centuries was overwhelmingly Roman Catholic in doctrine, ritual, calendar and clerical support before the Reformation. An uncontested general characterization.

claims.quote: Never since has the Roman Church attained to greater dignity, magnificence, or power.
claims.claim_text: The medieval papacy attained greater dignity, magnificence, and power at this period than at any time since.
claims.extractor_note: A sweeping comparative-historical claim; included as causal/checkable rather than purely interpretive since it makes a testable comparison across historical periods.

verification.rationale: The comparative judgment that the high-medieval papacy (12th-13th centuries, its temporal-power zenith) surpassed all later periods in political dominance over Europe is a widely held historical assessment; papal temporal supremacy declined after the 13th century (Avignon captivity, Western Schism, Reformation). The claim is defensible, though 'never since... greater magnificence' could be debated for the Renaissance papacy's splendor.

sources.title: Dictatus papae - Wikipedia
sources.note: Context for the high-medieval peak of papal power.

claims.quote: But “the noon of the papacy was the midnight of the world.”
claims.claim_text: It has been said that 'the noon of the papacy was the midnight of the world.'

verification.rationale: The quotation is genuine and correctly presented as a saying White is citing. It comes from J.A. Wylie's The History of Protestantism (vol. 1, p. 16, 1878) — one of the historical sources named in GC's references. As an accurately reproduced attributed saying, the quotation is verified (its truth-as-historical-judgment is a separate, evaluative matter, cf. GC-03-059/105).

sources.title: J.A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 1, p. 16 ('the noon of the Papacy was the midnight of the world')
sources.note: Locates the phrase in Wylie's History of Protestantism.

claims.quote: The Holy Scriptures were almost unknown, not only to the people, but to the priests.
claims.claim_text: During this period, the Holy Scriptures were almost unknown, not only to the laity but to the priests themselves.

verification.rationale: Low biblical literacy — including among many parish clergy — during parts of the early and central Middle Ages is documented; reform councils repeatedly complained of clerical ignorance, and vernacular access was limited. 'Almost unknown' is an overstatement (monastic and scholastic scholarship preserved and studied Scripture intensively), but the general claim about widespread ignorance of Scripture among ordinary clergy and laity is grounded.

sources.title: Council of Toulouse - Wikipedia
sources.note: Restricted lay access to Scripture; context of limited biblical availability.

claims.quote: For centuries Europe had made no progress in learning, arts, or civilization. A moral and intellectual paralysis had fallen upon Christendom.
claims.claim_text: For centuries under papal dominance, Europe made no progress in learning, arts, or civilization, and a moral and intellectual paralysis fell upon Christendom.

verification.rationale: The claim of centuries of total stagnation in learning, arts and civilization is the classic 'Dark Ages' thesis that modern historians reject. Scholarship documents substantial medieval developments — the Carolingian renewal, monastic preservation and copying of texts, the rise of universities, scholasticism, the 12th-century renaissance, Gothic architecture, agricultural and mechanical innovation. The sweeping 'no progress for centuries' matches 19th-century Protestant historiography (Wylie) rather than current scholarship.
verification.modern_consensus: The Middle Ages saw significant intellectual, artistic and technological development; the notion of centuries of uniform stagnation is rejected.
verification.period_historiography: J.A. Wylie's History of Protestantism and similar 19th-century works portrayed the medieval papal centuries as an era of intellectual darkness ('the midnight of the world').

sources.title: Dark Ages (historiography) - Wikipedia
sources.note: Historians reject the stagnation model implied by 'Dark Ages.'

sources.title: What the 'Dark Ages' Myth Gets Wrong About Medieval Science (TIME)
sources.note: Documents medieval innovation, contradicting 'no progress for centuries.'

claims.quote: Some of the reigning pontiffs were guilty of crimes so revolting that secular rulers endeavored to depose these dignitaries of the church as monsters too vile to be tolerated.
claims.claim_text: Some reigning popes of this period were guilty of crimes so revolting that secular rulers attempted to depose them as monsters too vile to be tolerated.

verification.rationale: The 10th-century 'pornocracy'/nadir of the papacy produced notoriously corrupt and violent popes (e.g. John XII, Benedict IX), and secular rulers did intervene to depose scandalous popes — Emperor Otto I deposed John XII (963), and Henry III deposed rival popes at Sutri (1046). The claim about revolting papal crimes and secular deposition is historically grounded.

sources.title: Dictatus papae - Wikipedia
sources.note: Reform context following the corrupt 10th-11th century papacy and imperial depositions.

claims.quote: “My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge: because thou hast rejected knowledge, I will also reject thee:... seeing thou hast forgotten the law of thy God, I will also forget thy children.” “There is no truth, nor mercy, nor knowledge of God in the land. By swearing, and lying, and killing, and stealing, and committing adultery, they break out, and blood toucheth blood.”
claims.claim_text: The prophet Hosea wrote: 'My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge...' and 'There is no truth, nor mercy, nor knowledge of God in the land. By swearing, and lying, and killing, and stealing, and committing adultery, they break out, and blood toucheth blood' (Hosea 4:1-2, 6).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Both quotations match the KJV: Hosea 4:6 ('My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge...') and Hosea 4:1-2 ('There is no truth, nor mercy, nor knowledge of God in the land...blood toucheth blood'). Quotations and attribution to Hosea accurate.

claims.quote: The condition of the world under the Romish power presented a fearful and striking fulfilment of the words of the prophet Hosea
claims.claim_text: The condition of the world under Roman Catholic power presented a fulfillment of the prophet Hosea's words about a people destroyed for lack of knowledge.

verification.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (was needs_verification:false). The overall assertion — that medieval conditions 'fulfilled' Hosea — is a prophetic-interpretive/theological claim, not empirically checkable. Control note: the one embedded checkable element, the attribution of the quoted words to Hosea, is accurate (verified at GC-03-107); no false factual assertion is smuggled into the framing. The 'fulfillment' judgment itself remains theological.
